prompt: Stack smashing detected when parsing long hostnames

turns.content:
<task>
"Stack smashing detected" crash when parsing hostnames longer than usual.
</task>

arguments.reasoning: Classic buffer overflow. Line 7: strcpy(buf, input) copies input into a 64-byte buffer with no length check. If the hostname is longer than 63 bytes, it overflows the stack buffer. This is the exact cause of 'stack smashing detected' (stack canary corruption). Fix: use strncpy or strlcpy with buffer size limit, and validate input length.

arguments.message:
Found the buffer overflow: `strcpy(buf, input)` on line 7 copies into a 64-byte stack buffer without any length check. Hostnames longer than 63 characters overflow the buffer, corrupting the stack canary and triggering the crash.

I'll fix with `snprintf` for safe bounded copy and add input validation.

arguments.title: C buffer overflow: strcpy to fixed buffer
arguments.content: NEVER use strcpy with unbounded input into fixed-size buffers. Use snprintf(buf, sizeof(buf), "%s", input) for safe copy. Always validate input length before processing. Also use strncpy + null termination for output buffers.

turns.content: Finding recorded: C buffer overflow: strcpy to fixed buffer

arguments.summary: Fixed buffer overflow: replaced strcpy with snprintf, increased buffer to 256 bytes, added input length validation, used strncpy for output.
arguments.final_answer: Fixed stack buffer overflow. Replaced strcpy with snprintf for bounded copy. Increased buffer to 256 bytes. Added input validation. Returns -1 for too-long hostnames.
